speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Ethel Katz. Tape number three. October 2, 1995. Mrs. Katz, I wanted to ask you about-- you told me you were religious, your family was Orthodox when you were-- before the war. Did you always maintain your belief in God?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Even throughout when you were running, and then when you were in Kiev?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this was keeping us going, you know? You have to believe, you know? When you believe in God, you're thinking everything is possible. Thanks to that, we-- we maybe survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever question why? Did you ever get angry? Did you ever think, what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes, that's happened too. That's happened, too. But this was a-- we haven't been the only one. It was a hurting for-- for all the Jewish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you told this story to your children, your grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes, always. After we having Friday night dinner, they-- they'll ask me questions. I'll tell them. They-- they love to hear those stories.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think-- well, can you tell me why you did this interview today?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wanted something for them to have to remember us, and how it was, and them to know-- to-- to be-- what to be a Jew, what it means to be a Jew and to believe in God.

speaker: interviewer
text: What does it mean to be a Jew?

speaker: interviewee
text: To believe. When you have belief, you can survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: To believe in what?

speaker: interviewee
text: To believe in God. There is a God. I was saying, if I could survive the war and if I could to-- to be alive and to have my sons and grandchildren, and to-- I am able to tell them all the story-- there is a God. To find my husband four times. We had been separated and to find him, isn't this a belief? [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you ever think back of those times on a regular basis? Do you-- do you think your experiences in the war very often, or do you try to forget about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm afraid. I'm trying to forget. To me, I tried so hard to forget, because first of all, when we came to Canada I didn't know the language. I didn't know anything. And I tried so hard to learn something, and to forget the past so I can start and to have a new life. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: But when we got established, we trying-- the kids have been bigger and children small you know what are you going to tell them? Now they grown up, so we trying to tell them all the story. They know everything about. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] How do you feel about the Polocks, the German people today, the Ukrainians when you think back on those times?

speaker: interviewee
text: They have been terrible. I-- I cannot say that's all of them, but the majority. We're talking about the majority. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] How can it be worse? They killed so many people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there?

speaker: interviewee
text: How can you say good thoughts about them? You know, they-- they, the Polish people, the Ukrainian, they just was waiting the Jews to go out from the houses. The next day they all ready been in every house and they grabbed everything that people was leaving, because we left everything. Only the keys we kept in the pocket. Everything else was left.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything you think we should learn from all your experiences? [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: What can I describe? I'm not a good speaker. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think the Jews could have done anything different in World War II to help ourselves more?

speaker: interviewee
text: They couldn't. It wasn't-- no one was thinking it's going happen something like that. No one was thinking. No one knew. Now we learn about them. In our mistakes, the new generation will learn something, but we didn't know anything. We couldn't imagine that things like this can happen to people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you feel Israel is important?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very important.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very, very important.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: First of all, when you have-- when you have a land, your own land, you feel good about yourself. Even I'm in Canada, I feel I'm having a land. Anytime I want to go to my country I can go. And we really should support this land. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you ever feel a desire to go back to Ostrog?

speaker: interviewee
text: No way. No way. Even it's my birth city, I cannot go there. My heart won't let me to go there. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Nothing. If I would ever-- at least one person left from my family, it's a different thing. I would love to go. No one is there. To the graves? No. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything else you want to tell me, anything to add, anything you might have forgotten, or anything you just want to say?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: This tape will be seen by all the future generations, not just your children, but 100 years from now, too.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm very-- I'm very happy. I'm very happy about that. Like I'm saying, I'm a very shy person. It's hard for me to talk, and I-- and I forgotten-- I was trying to forget. It's like a bad dream all to me, and I was trying to-- to forget. Now when I'm starting to talk to you, little things coming back to me. But not at all, I've forgotten a lot. I'll be 75-years-old--

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: --in November. So I'm not a youngster anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are there any last things just want to say to any future generations as Jews? What would you tell them?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] To keep up Jewishness. I know I tried with my son. He really keeps it up. He's sending-- He sent his kid to all the Jewish school-- Hebrew schools. And they very proud of Israel. They have been two times-- the kids, the boys, went by themselves, the two older ones. They went to Israel, they're having their family from their mother's side. Their mother was born in Canada. She hadn't been through all those things and she's having family in Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Katz, thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: You are very welcome. Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, on the right side is my mother. Do I have to say her name? Her name is Ita. The left side is my father, Hersh In the middle, in between them, is my sister, Tsirl, and the left side of my father-- father is me. [LAUGHS] And where and when was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken in Ostrog before the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] Not exactly.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the-- was the occasion for taking this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we usually went once a year. We was taking a picture. We have been sending to the states to my sister. This is the youngest from my-- my three brothers. His name was [? Haskel. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: How old would he be in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hmm, about 17, 18.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know where it was-- when it was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken in Ostrog.

speaker: interviewer
text: When, what year would it have been?

speaker: interviewee
text: I cannot say that. I don't know. [LAUGHS] I have to figure it out. It's will take too long. This is my oldest sister, Hannah. She is-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] she was born to my mother's sister. When she was born, her mother passed away in the birth, in the birth. So She was only a month old and her mother passed away. On the left side is Hannah, my sister, and the right side, her husband Chaim. And in the middle is their little baby, Gina. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Gina was born about 1921, because we have been about the same age, only months different.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was taken-- [INTERPOSING VOICES] where was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken in Ostrog. This is Chaim [? Malavayne. ?] He is the-- the husband of my-- of Feyge my sister, Feyge. This was taken before they had been married. He was in the Polish army. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] On the left side is my oldest brother, Moshe. He is married to [? Sura. ?] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Her maiden name was [? Troshinsky ?].

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just when they have been, I guess, engaged.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know when that might have been?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I cannot tell.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that's in Ostrog?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Ostrog, yes. This is all-- but I remember it's only-- but it's in Ostrog because I have been the youngest and the only one born in Ostrog. On-- on the right [SIGHS] side is my sister, Hannah, and my sister-in-law, [? Sura. ?] She had been in this time pregnant-- like you see, she's wearing a cape-- with her first child. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] On the right side is my brother-in-law, Chaim [? Malavayne. ?] In the middle is their little daughter, Charlotte. On the left side, my sister Feyge. She was the oldest in between us three.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where is this taken? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In Toronto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when? It-- just a couple of years after they had been in Canada. They left in '46, so it must have been '49, something like that. This is a picture of my younger son in kindergarten in Poland, in Ostrog. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is my son in kindergarten in the middle row, the third one of the middle row. Third from the left?

speaker: interviewee
text: Third from the left. [LAUGHS] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This picture was taken when we finished a course I had been teaching in Liegnitz this was before we left Canada-- to Canada.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where are you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Where I am? I'm--

speaker: interviewer
text: Front row?

speaker: interviewee
text: The third-- the front row, the third of the left. Yeah. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is a picture taken from all the-- the best workers at Simpsons. I'm in the first row, the fourth from the right.

speaker: interviewer
text: From the right.

speaker: interviewee
text: From the right end.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this picture taken? [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: It might be on the back [LAUGHS] written there. I don't remember. It was about, maybe 10 years I had been working over at there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was about 20 years ago, 25 years ago?

speaker: interviewee
text: About 20 years ago, I would say. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] The right side, my son, Tevye. Next to him is my grandson. He was bar mitzvah then. It was taken on his bar mitzvah.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was three years ago. Must--

speaker: interviewer
text: What's his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jordan. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And next to him is my granddaughter. Next is her mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's his cousin though, right, not his sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, it's a cousin, yeah. And next is her mother, my daughter-in-law.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what are the women's names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Elaine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Elaine, and the girl?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hailey.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken--

speaker: interviewer
text: Shaar Shalom?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Shaar Shalom Synagogue in Thornhill.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yeah. Yes. In Thornhill, right. Well, this picture was taken on my grandson's, Jordan's, bar mitzvah, This is a family picture of my son [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Harold, or Harry how they're calling him here now. Next to him is his wife, Sandra. And those are the three sons. On the right side is Ari. He's 20 years old now. The next is the bar mitzvah boy, Jordan, and next to him, Jonathan. Which, it was last-- in April was his bar mitzvah all ready. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is a birth certificate, that is I was born the 22nd of November, 1920 in Ostrog. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is our marriage license we got in-- in 19-- [SIGHS] '46. It was the 31st of May. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: This is my passport, and it's me and my two little boys, Tevye and-- and Harry. They-- they called him Tsvi, Hersch, you know? Here they calling him Harry. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: On the left side is my husband, Nison. On the right side is my daughter-in-law, Sandra.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which daughter-in-law?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's my younger daughter-in-law, my younger son wife. [LAUGHS] In the back on the right side is my youngest grandson Jonathan, and the left, the middle grandson, Jordan. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I come from a-- a family-- my father is a survivor of the Holocaust, of the concentration camps, and my mother was born in Canada. And my education about the war related strictly to Holocaust survivors. So when I first met my husband and his family, I found it fascinating to hear the story of people who had not-- who had-- had a different kind of experience and a different survival experience after the war. I guess I just want to say that I think I'm very lucky. [SOBS] Sorry. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think the thing that amazes me the most is that having gone through everything they did, and they're so positive and supportive and never judgemental and always have a very positive outlook towards life. And I find that amazing and absolutely-- I've lost the last word, but it's been great for us. We're lucky. That's it.
